questions.explanation: Under a race-notice recording statute, a subsequent purchaser prevails over a prior grantee only if the subsequent purchaser (1) took without notice (actual, constructive, or inquiry) of the prior unrecorded conveyance and (2) records first — here, the second buyer paid value, had no notice of the first unrecorded deed, and recorded promptly, satisfying both race-notice requirements, so he prevails over the first grantee who never recorded. This outcome would differ from a pure race statute (irrelevant here) or notice statute (which wouldn't require recording first, but is not what's described), so the 'delivery alone always wins' claim in option B misstates recording act priorities meant to protect good-faith subsequent purchasers; there is no familial relationship requirement in recording statutes; and while the grantor's fraud may give the first grantee a claim against the grantor personally, it does not by itself override the second buyer's protected bona fide purchaser status under the recording act as applied to the property itself.

questions.stem: A landowner grants his neighbor a right to run a water pipeline underneath the landowner's property to reach the neighbor's well, memorialized in a signed and recorded written agreement described as "an easement appurtenant to the neighbor's parcel." The neighbor later sells her parcel to a new owner. Does the new owner automatically receive the benefit of the pipeline easement?
choices: Yes, because an easement appurtenant benefits a particular parcel of land (the dominant estate) and automatically transfers with that parcel upon conveyance, regardless of whether the deed to the new owner specifically mentions the easement.
No, because easements are always personal to the original party and can never be transferred to a subsequent owner of the dominant parcel.
No, because the new owner must execute a brand-new easement agreement directly with the servient landowner for the easement to continue.
Yes, but only if the new owner pays additional separate consideration to the servient landowner for continued use.
questions.explanation: An easement appurtenant is attached to and benefits a particular parcel of land (the dominant estate) rather than a specific person, and it automatically passes with the dominant estate upon transfer to subsequent owners, without needing to be specifically mentioned in the new deed or re-negotiated. This directly contradicts the notion that easements are always merely personal (that description fits an easement in gross, not an easement appurtenant as described here), there is no need for a brand-new agreement since appurtenant easements run with the land by operation of law, and no additional consideration from the new owner is required for the appurtenant benefit to continue transferring.

questions.stem: A landowner owns a large parcel and subdivides it into 20 residential lots, recording a subdivision plan and separate deeds for each lot that include an identical restrictive covenant limiting each lot "to single-family residential use only." A buyer of one lot later attempts to open a small retail shop on his lot, and a neighboring lot owner (who also bought subject to the same covenant) sues to enforce the restriction. Can the neighboring lot owner, who is not the original developer, enforce the covenant under the doctrine of equitable servitudes?
choices: Yes, likely, because when a common scheme of development imposes uniform covenants on all lots in a subdivision, courts recognize reciprocal negative easements/equitable servitudes enforceable by any lot owner within the common scheme, even though not a party to the original covenant.
No, because only the original subdividing developer, and no subsequent lot owner, may ever enforce a restrictive covenant.
No, because restrictive covenants limiting land use are always void as unreasonable restraints on alienation.
Yes, but only if the neighboring lot owner's own deed happens to lack the identical restriction, since matching restrictions cancel each other out.
questions.explanation: Where a common grantor establishes a general/common scheme of development with uniform restrictive covenants recorded and imposed on all lots in a subdivision, courts enforce reciprocal negative easements (equitable servitudes) among the lot owners themselves, allowing any owner within the scheme to enforce the restriction against another owner, even though they are not the original covenanting parties, because the mutual benefit and burden runs with each lot. This means enforcement is not limited to only the original developer (option B misstates the doctrine's core purpose, which is horizontal enforceability among lot owners), reasonable land-use restrictions like single-family-use covenants are not void as restraints on alienation (they restrict use, not the ability to transfer title, and are routinely enforced), and there is no rule that matching identical restrictions somehow cancel each other's enforceability — quite the opposite, uniformity is what supports the common scheme doctrine.

questions.stem: A driver runs a red light and collides with another car, injuring the other driver. At trial, the plaintiff proves the defendant ran the red light but presents no direct evidence about how fast the defendant was traveling or exactly how the collision occurred beyond the light violation. The jurisdiction recognizes negligence per se for traffic statute violations. What is the effect of proving the statutory violation?
choices: The statutory violation establishes duty and breach (negligence per se), but the plaintiff must still prove that the violation actually and proximately caused the plaintiff's injury and that the plaintiff falls within the class the statute was designed to protect.
The statutory violation alone automatically establishes the defendant's total liability for all damages without any further proof of causation.
Negligence per se is irrelevant because criminal traffic violations can never be used in a civil negligence case.
The statutory violation creates only a rebuttable presumption of contributory negligence on the part of the plaintiff.
questions.explanation: Negligence per se allows an unexcused violation of a statute (like a traffic law) designed to protect against the type of harm suffered by a plaintiff within the protected class to conclusively or presumptively establish duty and breach, but the plaintiff must still independently prove causation (actual and proximate) and damages to prevail — negligence per se does not eliminate the causation element. It does not establish total liability automatically without proof of causation, criminal statutes are routinely and properly used to establish the standard of care in civil negligence per se analysis (so the blanket exclusion is wrong), and negligence per se addresses the defendant's breach of duty, not a presumption regarding the plaintiff's own contributory negligence.

questions.stem: A manufacturer sells a power saw with a defectively designed blade guard that fails to stay in place during normal use, and a purchaser is injured when the guard slips and his hand contacts the blade during ordinary operation. The purchaser sues under a strict products liability theory. Which of the following must the purchaser prove to prevail?
choices: That the product was defective when it left the manufacturer's control, that the defect made the product unreasonably dangerous, and that the defect caused the plaintiff's injury while the product was being used in a reasonably foreseeable manner — without needing to prove the manufacturer was negligent.
That the manufacturer was negligent in its design process, since strict liability claims require the same proof of fault as an ordinary negligence claim.
That the purchaser had privity of contract directly with the manufacturer, since privity is required for any product liability claim.
That the manufacturer intended for the product to cause harm, since strict liability requires proof of intent.
questions.explanation: Strict products liability requires proof that the product was defective (in this case, a design defect) when it left the defendant's control, that the defect rendered the product unreasonably dangerous, and that the defect was the actual and proximate cause of injury during reasonably foreseeable use — critically, the plaintiff need not prove the manufacturer's negligence or fault, which is the entire point of strict liability as distinguished from negligence-based claims. This eliminates the need to prove negligence (contrary to option B), privity of contract has been abolished as a requirement for product liability claims brought by ultimate users/consumers under modern law (contrary to option C), and strict liability does not require proof of intent to harm, which would instead describe an intentional tort (contrary to option D).

questions.stem: A store owner notices a customer he reasonably (but mistakenly) believes has shoplifted merchandise. He detains the customer in a back office for twenty minutes while investigating, using reasonable, non-excessive force, and it later turns out the customer was innocent. The customer sues for false imprisonment. Under the shopkeeper's privilege recognized in most jurisdictions, what is the most likely outcome?
choices: The store owner likely has a valid defense, because the shopkeeper's privilege permits a reasonable, good-faith detention on reasonable suspicion of shoplifting, conducted in a reasonable manner and for a reasonable time to investigate, even if the suspicion later proves mistaken.
The store owner is automatically liable for false imprisonment because any detention of an innocent customer is unlawful regardless of the surrounding circumstances.
The store owner is liable because the shopkeeper's privilege only applies once a shoplifting charge has actually been formally filed with police.
The store owner has no defense because the privilege requires the detained customer to actually be guilty of the underlying offense.
questions.explanation: The shopkeeper's privilege is a recognized defense to false imprisonment (and related torts) that allows a merchant to detain a suspected shoplifter for a reasonable time, in a reasonable manner, and based on reasonable grounds for suspicion, even if it later turns out the suspicion was mistaken and the person was innocent, because the law does not require certainty of guilt to justify a brief, reasonable investigatory detention. This means an innocent customer's detention is not automatically unlawful if these reasonableness requirements are met (contrary to option B), the privilege does not require a formal charge to have been filed (it is meant to apply during the investigatory stage before involving police, if at all), and actual guilt is not required for the privilege to apply — reasonable, good-faith mistaken suspicion suffices (contrary to option D).

questions.stem: A plaintiff sues a defendant for negligence after a car accident. At trial, evidence shows the plaintiff was also partly at fault by failing to signal a lane change, contributing 20% to the accident, while the defendant, who was speeding, is found 80% at fault. The jurisdiction follows pure comparative negligence. The jury finds the plaintiff's total damages to be $100,000. How much can the plaintiff recover?
choices: $80,000, because under pure comparative negligence, the plaintiff's recovery is reduced by his own percentage of fault (20%), regardless of whether his fault exceeds the defendant's.
$0, because any degree of contributory fault by the plaintiff completely bars recovery under pure comparative negligence.
$100,000, because comparative negligence never reduces a plaintiff's total recovery when the defendant is found more than 50% at fault.
$20,000, because the plaintiff may only recover an amount equal to his own percentage of fault.
questions.explanation: Under pure comparative negligence, a plaintiff's damages award is reduced in proportion to his own percentage of fault, but he may still recover the remaining percentage of damages even if his fault is very high (up to 99%), meaning here the plaintiff recovers 80% of $100,000 (his damages reduced by his own 20% fault) = $80,000. This contradicts the old common-law pure contributory negligence rule (which would bar all recovery for any plaintiff fault, described in option B, but that is not the 'pure comparative negligence' rule at issue here), it is not true that fault percentages don't affect recovery when the defendant is more than 50% at fault (that describes modified/partial comparative negligence jurisdictions' threshold rule, not pure comparative negligence, and even then the calculation would still reduce recovery by the plaintiff's fault percentage, not eliminate the reduction), and the plaintiff recovers based on the defendant's share of fault applied to total damages, not simply an amount equal to the plaintiff's own fault percentage.

questions.stem: A homeowner has a large oak tree on her property with a visibly rotted, cracking branch hanging directly over the public sidewalk. She is aware of the branch's condition but takes no action. The branch falls and injures a pedestrian walking on the sidewalk. The pedestrian sues the homeowner for negligence. What is the most accurate assessment of the homeowner's potential liability?
choices: The homeowner may be liable because a landowner who has actual knowledge of a dangerous condition on her property that poses an unreasonable risk of harm to those outside the property, such as pedestrians on an adjacent public sidewalk, generally has a duty to take reasonable steps to eliminate the danger.
The homeowner cannot be liable because landowners owe no duty whatsoever to anyone located outside the boundaries of their own property.
The homeowner cannot be liable because natural conditions on land, such as trees, can never give rise to any liability regardless of the landowner's knowledge of the danger.
The homeowner is strictly liable regardless of her knowledge of the branch's condition, since falling tree branches are treated as abnormally dangerous activities.
questions.explanation: The modern and majority rule recognizes that a landowner (especially in urban/developed areas) who has actual knowledge of a dangerous condition on her property (natural or artificial) that creates an unreasonable risk of harm to persons outside the property, such as pedestrians on an adjoining sidewalk, owes a duty to take reasonable steps to remedy or warn of the danger, and this is a well-established modern exception to any old blanket no-duty rule. Landowners are not entirely free from duties to those outside their property under the modern approach (contradicting option B), natural conditions no longer categorically escape liability especially once actual knowledge of the specific danger is shown (undermining the traditional common-law 'natural condition' immunity relied upon in option C), and this scenario is analyzed under ordinary negligence principles rather than strict liability for abnormally dangerous activities, which requires a different showing altogether (undermining option D).

questions.stem: A defendant, angry during an argument, raises his fist as if to strike the plaintiff, who reasonably believes she is about to be hit and flinches backward, though the defendant never actually makes physical contact and stops himself. The plaintiff sues for assault. Which of the following best describes whether the tort of assault is established?
choices: Assault is established because the tort requires only that the defendant intentionally caused a reasonable apprehension of imminent harmful or offensive contact, and actual physical contact is not required.
Assault requires actual physical contact with the plaintiff's body, so no assault occurred here since the defendant never struck her.
Assault requires proof that the plaintiff suffered a physical injury as a result of the encounter.
Assault cannot occur unless the defendant actually intended to and did successfully strike the plaintiff.
questions.explanation: The tort of assault requires an intentional act that causes the plaintiff a reasonable apprehension of imminent harmful or offensive contact, and it is specifically distinguished from battery in that assault does not require actual physical contact — the reasonable apprehension of imminent contact is itself the actionable harm. This means the absence of physical contact does not defeat the claim (contradicting option B, which actually describes battery's contact requirement, not assault's), no physical injury is required to establish assault since the tort protects against the apprehension itself (contradicting option C), and a completed/successful strike is not required — indeed, if contact had occurred, the claim would more properly be characterized as battery rather than assault (contradicting option D).

questions.stem: A commercial airline negligently loses a passenger's luggage containing rare, irreplaceable family photographs. The passenger sues for negligent infliction of emotional distress, claiming severe grief over the loss of the photographs, but suffered no physical impact, no physical symptoms, and was never in any zone of physical danger. Under the majority approach to negligent infliction of emotional distress, is the passenger likely to prevail on this claim?
choices: No, because most jurisdictions require either a physical impact, physical manifestation of the distress, or presence within a zone of danger (or a close relationship to an endangered person under bystander recovery rules), none of which is present here for a claim arising from mere property loss.
Yes, because emotional distress claims never require any physical component when the plaintiff's distress is genuine and severe.
Yes, because any negligent loss of sentimentally valuable property automatically supports a negligent infliction of emotional distress claim.
No, because negligent infliction of emotional distress claims may never be brought by airline passengers under any circumstances.
questions.explanation: Most jurisdictions limit negligent infliction of emotional distress claims through requirements such as physical impact, physical symptoms/manifestation of the distress, presence within a zone of physical danger, or (for bystanders) a close relationship to a person who was physically endangered or injured — mere loss of sentimentally valuable property, without any physical impact, symptom, or danger, generally does not satisfy these limiting doctrines in the majority of jurisdictions. This contradicts the notion that no physical component is ever required (some limiting factor is typically required in the majority approach, undermining option B), rules out an automatic claim based solely on sentimental property loss (undermining option C), and there's no categorical rule that airline passengers specifically can never bring such claims — the limitation here arises from the general elements of the tort, not the nature of the defendant's business (undermining option D).

questions.stem: A construction company uses dynamite to demolish an old building in a densely populated urban area. Despite taking every reasonable precaution, a chunk of debris is propelled onto a neighboring property, damaging a parked car. The car owner sues the construction company. Under the doctrine governing abnormally dangerous activities, what standard of liability applies?
choices: Strict liability applies, because blasting with explosives is generally treated as an abnormally dangerous activity, and liability attaches for resulting harm regardless of the degree of care exercised.
Ordinary negligence applies, and the construction company will escape liability entirely because it exercised every reasonable precaution.
No liability applies because acts of demolition are always classified as governmental or public benefit activities exempt from tort liability.
Liability requires proof that the construction company intended to cause damage to neighboring property.
questions.explanation: Blasting/use of explosives is a paradigmatic example of an abnormally dangerous (ultrahazardous) activity subject to strict liability under Restatement principles, meaning the defendant is liable for resulting harm even if reasonable care was exercised, because the activity carries a high degree of risk that cannot be eliminated by care and is not a matter of common usage in the area. This means the exercise of reasonable care does not provide a defense the way it would in an ordinary negligence claim (contradicting option B, which misapplies a negligence framework), demolition/blasting is not exempted from tort liability merely because it may occur in connection with development or construction projects (contradicting option C, which invents an inapplicable exemption), and strict liability for abnormally dangerous activities does not require any proof of intent to harm (contradicting option D, which would instead describe an intentional tort standard).

questions.stem: A plaintiff sues multiple defendants for defamation after a newspaper published a false statement claiming the plaintiff, a private individual (not a public figure), embezzled funds from his employer, when in fact no embezzlement occurred. The statement was published due to the reporter's carelessness in failing to verify the source, but not with knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard for the truth. Under the constitutional standard applicable to private-figure plaintiffs on matters of public concern, what must the plaintiff prove regarding fault?
choices: At minimum, negligence regarding the truth or falsity of the statement, because Gertz v. Robert Welch established that private figures suing on matters of public concern need only prove negligence, not the higher actual malice standard required of public figures/officials.
Actual malice (knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard for the truth), because all defamation plaintiffs, whether public or private figures, must meet this identical constitutional standard.
Strict liability applies automatically to any false statement printed by a newspaper regardless of the plaintiff's public or private status.
No fault standard applies at all because defamation is a strict liability tort with no constitutional fault requirement in any context.
questions.explanation: Under Gertz v. Robert Welch, Inc., private-figure plaintiffs suing for defamation regarding matters of public concern are constitutionally required to prove at least negligence as to the truth or falsity of the statement (states may impose a higher standard, but negligence is the constitutional floor), which is a lower bar than the 'actual malice' (knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard) standard imposed on public officials/figures under New York Times v. Sullivan. This means the actual malice standard is not uniformly required of all plaintiffs regardless of public/private status (contradicting option B, which conflates the public-figure and private-figure standards), strict liability without any fault requirement was rejected by the Supreme Court for matters of public concern (contradicting option C), and there is indeed a constitutional fault requirement imposed on defamation claims involving matters of public concern, contrary to option D's blanket denial.

questions.stem: A plaintiff, a citizen of State A, sues a defendant, a citizen of State B, in federal district court, alleging state-law breach of contract claims and seeking $100,000 in damages. The defendant argues the federal court lacks subject matter jurisdiction. Assuming the amount in controversy requirement is satisfied, is the federal court likely to have subject matter jurisdiction?
choices: Yes, because complete diversity exists between the plaintiff and defendant (citizens of different states) and the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000, satisfying diversity jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. Section 1332.
No, because federal courts can never hear cases involving only state-law claims under any circumstances.
No, because diversity jurisdiction requires that all claims arise under federal law, not state law.
Yes, but only if the defendant affirmatively consents in writing to federal jurisdiction.
questions.explanation: Diversity jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. Section 1332 permits federal courts to hear state-law claims when there is complete diversity of citizenship between all plaintiffs and all defendants and the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs — this is precisely the scenario presented, so jurisdiction is proper. Federal courts routinely hear state-law claims via diversity jurisdiction (contradicting option B's absolute claim), diversity jurisdiction is specifically designed for state-law claims between diverse citizens rather than requiring a federal question (contradicting option C, which describes federal question jurisdiction instead), and diversity jurisdiction does not require the defendant's consent — it exists based on the statutory citizenship and amount-in-controversy requirements alone (contradicting option D).

questions.stem: A plaintiff files a complaint in federal court alleging only a bare legal conclusion that "the defendant acted negligently and caused the plaintiff's injuries," without any supporting factual allegations describing what the defendant did, when, or how the injury occurred. The defendant moves to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6). How should the court likely rule?
choices: The court should likely grant the motion because, under the plausibility standard of Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly and Ashcroft v. Iqbal, a complaint must allege sufficient factual content to state a claim that is plausible on its face, and bare legal conclusions unsupported by facts are insufficient.
The court should deny the motion because any complaint that recites the legal elements of a cause of action automatically survives a motion to dismiss.
The court should deny the motion because Rule 12(b)(6) motions are only available in cases involving federal questions, not state-law negligence claims.
The court should grant the motion, but only because the plaintiff failed to attach supporting evidence and exhibits to the complaint.
questions.explanation: Under the Twombly/Iqbal plausibility pleading standard now governing federal pleading under Rule 8(a) and tested via Rule 12(b)(6), a complaint must contain sufficient factual matter, accepted as true, to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face; mere legal conclusions or a formulaic recitation of a cause of action's elements, without supporting facts, are not entitled to the presumption of truth and are insufficient to survive dismissal. This means reciting legal elements alone does not guarantee survival (contradicting option B), Rule 12(b)(6) applies to all civil actions in federal court regardless of the jurisdictional basis, including diversity-based state-law claims (contradicting option C), and federal pleading under Rule 8 does not require attaching evidentiary exhibits at the pleading stage — it requires plausible factual allegations, not evidentiary proof (contradicting option D).

questions.stem: A plaintiff sues a defendant in federal court and properly serves the summons and complaint. The defendant, instead of filing an answer, wants to challenge personal jurisdiction, improper venue, and insufficient service of process, while also asserting a failure-to-state-a-claim defense, all before filing a responsive answer. Under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, what is the proper mechanism for raising these defenses together?
choices: The defendant may raise all of these defenses together in a single pre-answer motion under Rule 12(b), and defenses like personal jurisdiction, venue, and service of process are waived if not raised in this initial motion (or the answer, if no such motion is made).
The defendant must file a separate lawsuit to challenge personal jurisdiction before responding to the current complaint in any way.
The defendant must raise each defense in a series of entirely separate, sequential motions filed on different dates.
The defendant automatically waives all listed defenses simply by requesting an extension of time to respond to the complaint.
questions.explanation: Rule 12(b) permits a defendant to consolidate multiple defenses—including lack of personal jurisdiction, improper venue, insufficient process/service, and failure to state a claim—into a single pre-answer motion, and Rule 12(h) provides that certain defenses (personal jurisdiction, venue, service) are waived if omitted from that initial Rule 12 motion or the answer if no such motion is filed, encouraging consolidation of these defenses at the earliest opportunity. There is no requirement (or mechanism) to file a wholly separate lawsuit merely to contest personal jurisdiction (contradicting option B), the rules specifically favor consolidating these defenses into one motion rather than filing them sequentially in separate motions (contradicting option C), and merely requesting a routine extension of time to respond does not, by itself, waive these defenses (contradicting option D).

questions.stem: A plaintiff files a personal injury lawsuit in federal court against a defendant based on diversity jurisdiction. During discovery, the plaintiff serves a set of interrogatories asking the defendant to describe, in detail, all facts supporting each affirmative defense raised in the defendant's answer. The defendant objects, arguing this seeks legal conclusions and attorney work product. Is the defendant's blanket objection likely to succeed?
choices: No, because interrogatories asking a party to state the facts underlying its claims or defenses (a "contention interrogatory") are generally permissible discovery devices under Rule 33, even though the responding party may need to apply facts to legal standards in answering.
Yes, because interrogatories can never properly ask about the factual basis for an affirmative defense under any circumstances.
Yes, because any interrogatory that touches on a party's legal position is automatically protected work product with no exceptions.
No, but only because interrogatories are limited exclusively to questions about a party's identity and contact information.
questions.explanation: Contention interrogatories, which ask a party to state the facts supporting its claims or defenses, are a well-recognized and generally permissible discovery tool under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 33, even though answering them may require the responding party to apply law to fact to some degree; courts may sometimes defer such interrogatories until later in discovery but do not categorically bar them. A blanket objection based purely on the interrogatory touching legal conclusions or defenses does not automatically succeed (contradicting option B's categorical bar), the work-product doctrine protects attorney mental impressions and litigation strategy materials, not a party's obligation to disclose the factual basis for its own pleaded defenses (contradicting option C's blanket work-product claim), and interrogatories are not limited merely to identity/contact information — they are a broad discovery tool covering any non-privileged matter relevant to a claim or defense (contradicting option D).

questions.stem: A plaintiff sues a defendant in federal court, and the case proceeds to a jury trial. After the plaintiff presents her case-in-chief and rests, the defendant moves for judgment as a matter of law under Rule 50(a), arguing the plaintiff has failed to present legally sufficient evidence for a reasonable jury to find in her favor on an essential element of her claim. What standard should the court apply in ruling on this motion?
choices: The court must view the evidence in the light most favorable to the plaintiff (the non-moving party) and grant the motion only if no reasonable jury could find for the plaintiff on the evidence presented.
The court must weigh the credibility of witnesses and decide which side's evidence it personally finds more persuasive before ruling.
The court must automatically grant the motion whenever the defendant disputes any factual assertion made by the plaintiff.
The court may not rule on this motion until after the jury has already returned its verdict.
questions.explanation: In ruling on a Rule 50(a) motion for judgment as a matter of law made during trial, the court must view all the evidence in the light most favorable to the non-moving party (here, the plaintiff), draw all reasonable inferences in that party's favor, and grant the motion only if a reasonable jury would not have a legally sufficient evidentiary basis to find for that party — the court does not weigh credibility or resolve factual disputes, which remain jury functions. This means the court does not itself weigh credibility or decide which evidence is more persuasive (contradicting option B, which describes fact-finding, not the JMOL standard), the motion is not automatically granted merely because facts are disputed — genuine factual disputes on which reasonable minds could differ preclude JMOL (contradicting option C), and a Rule 50(a) motion is specifically designed to be raised and ruled upon during trial before the case is submitted to the jury, not only after a verdict (which would instead implicate a Rule 50(b) renewed motion, contradicting option D).

questions.stem: A plaintiff sues a defendant for breach of contract in federal court based on diversity jurisdiction, and loses after a full trial on the merits, with judgment entered for the defendant. The plaintiff later files a new lawsuit against the same defendant in a different federal court, asserting a different legal theory (fraud) but based on the exact same underlying transaction and occurrence. The defendant argues the new suit is barred. Under the doctrine of claim preclusion (res judicata), is the defendant likely correct?
choices: Yes, likely, because claim preclusion bars relitigation not only of claims actually raised but also of claims that could have been raised arising from the same transaction or occurrence, once a final judgment on the merits has been entered between the same parties.
No, because claim preclusion only bars a second lawsuit if it asserts the exact identical legal theory as the first lawsuit.
No, because claim preclusion never applies unless the second lawsuit is filed in the exact same court as the first.
Yes, but only if the plaintiff's first lawsuit resulted in a settlement rather than a final judgment after trial.
questions.explanation: Claim preclusion (res judicata) bars a party from relitigating claims that were actually raised, as well as claims that could have been raised, arising from the same transaction or occurrence in a prior action that concluded in a final judgment on the merits between the same parties (or their privies); most jurisdictions and federal common law apply a transactional test, so a new legal theory (fraud) based on the identical underlying transaction is barred if it could have been asserted in the earlier suit. This means claim preclusion is not limited to identical legal theories — it reaches all claims arising from the same transactional nucleus of facts, not just the same theory (contradicting option B), it does not require the second suit be filed in the same court (claim preclusion travels with the judgment regardless of forum, contradicting option C), and it is specifically a final judgment after trial on the merits (as occurred here) — not merely a settlement — that is the classic trigger for claim preclusion, making option D's requirement backward.

questions.stem: A plaintiff files suit in federal court against a defendant, invoking diversity jurisdiction. The defendant, believing the plaintiff filed in an inconvenient forum despite venue being technically proper, wants to have the case moved to a different federal district where the case could also have been brought and where witnesses and evidence are located. Under 28 U.S.C. Section 1404(a), what is the proper procedural mechanism?
choices: The defendant may move to transfer venue to the more convenient district, and the court will weigh private and public interest factors such as witness convenience, access to evidence, and the interests of justice, since Section 1404(a) permits transfer between federal districts when venue is proper in both.
The defendant must file a motion to dismiss for improper venue, since Section 1404(a) only allows dismissal, not transfer.
The defendant has no available remedy because a plaintiff's initial choice of any technically proper venue can never be challenged or altered.
The defendant must obtain the plaintiff's written consent before any transfer motion may even be filed with the court.
questions.explanation: 28 U.S.C. Section 1404(a) allows a federal court to transfer a civil action to another district or division where venue would also be proper, for the convenience of parties and witnesses and in the interest of justice, and courts apply a multi-factor balancing test considering private factors (witness/evidence access, convenience) and public factors (court congestion, local interest, choice of law considerations) — this is precisely the mechanism for the scenario described, distinct from dismissal for improper venue under Section 1406. Section 1404(a) is a transfer statute, not a dismissal statute (contradicting option B, which confuses it with Section 1406(a)'s remedy for improper venue), venue transfer motions are a well-established procedural tool available even when the original venue is technically proper (contradicting option C's absolute bar), and no plaintiff consent is required before a defendant may file a transfer motion — the court decides the motion based on the balancing factors (contradicting option D).

questions.stem: A plaintiff and a defendant are both citizens of State A. The plaintiff sues the defendant in federal court solely on a state-law tort claim, with no federal question involved, asserting only that the case satisfies diversity jurisdiction because the plaintiff recently moved to State B a few months before filing suit, intending to return to State A after the litigation concludes. The defendant challenges subject matter jurisdiction. What is the most likely outcome?
choices: The court likely lacks diversity jurisdiction because citizenship for diversity purposes requires domicile, meaning a fixed habitation with intent to remain indefinitely, and a temporary relocation with an announced intent to return to State A undermines the claim of a genuine change in domicile to State B.
The court has diversity jurisdiction automatically because physical presence in a different state at the time of filing is the sole requirement for citizenship, regardless of intent.
The court has diversity jurisdiction because federal courts must accept a plaintiff's own assertion of citizenship without any further inquiry.
The court lacks jurisdiction only because the claim arises under state tort law rather than federal law.
questions.explanation: For diversity jurisdiction purposes, an individual's citizenship is determined by domicile, which requires both physical presence in a state and the intent to remain there indefinitely (not merely temporarily or for the duration of litigation); a plaintiff who moves with an announced intent to return to the original state after the lawsuit concludes has likely not established a new domicile, meaning complete diversity would be lacking since both parties would be deemed citizens of State A. Physical presence alone, without the requisite intent to remain indefinitely, is not sufficient to establish a new domicile (contradicting option B), courts independently scrutinize and may reject a plaintiff's self-serving citizenship assertions, especially where jurisdictional manipulation is suspected (contradicting option C), and the absence of a federal question is entirely consistent with proper diversity jurisdiction in other circumstances — the actual jurisdictional defect here lies in the lack of complete diversity, not the state-law nature of the claim itself (contradicting option D's reasoning).

questions.stem: A plaintiff brings a claim in federal court against a defendant for breach of contract. The defendant believes it has a related claim against a third party who is not currently part of the lawsuit, arising from the same contract, and wants to bring that third party into the existing case to seek indemnification for any liability the defendant might owe the plaintiff. What is the proper procedural mechanism for the defendant to accomplish this?
choices: The defendant may file a third-party complaint under Rule 14, impleading the third party as a defendant to the extent that third party may be liable to the original defendant for all or part of the plaintiff's claim against it.
The defendant must wait until the underlying lawsuit is fully resolved and then file an entirely separate, independent lawsuit against the third party.
The defendant cannot bring in any additional party under any circumstances once the original lawsuit has already been filed by the plaintiff.
The defendant may only add the third party by amending the complaint itself, a step that only the plaintiff, not the defendant, is permitted to take.
questions.explanation: Rule 14 governs third-party practice (impleader), allowing a defending party to bring in a third party who may be liable to it for all or part of the claim asserted against it by the original plaintiff, such as an indemnification or contribution claim, all within the same lawsuit rather than requiring a separate action. This means the defendant need not wait for a separate later lawsuit (contradicting option B, though it remains available as an alternative in some circumstances, impleader within the same suit is the proper and efficient Rule 14 mechanism), joinder mechanisms like impleader are specifically designed to allow additional parties to be brought into an already-filed lawsuit (contradicting option C's absolute bar), and a defendant seeking to bring in a third party for indemnification files its own third-party complaint under Rule 14 rather than needing to amend the plaintiff's original complaint, a device that also is not the exclusive province of the plaintiff in the way option D suggests.
